5.1.q: 1 (a). Describe one feature of aseptic surgery in the early 20th century. (2 marks)
5.1.m: Model Answer: One feature of aseptic surgery was the use of steam sterilisation. (1 mark) Hospitals began using an autoclave machine, invented in 1881, to thoroughly sterilise all surgical instruments in boiling steam before an operation began. (1 mark)

5.2.q: 1 (b). Describe one feature of early x-ray machines. (2 marks)
5.2.m: Model Answer: One feature of early x-ray machines was that they took a very long time to produce an image. (1 mark) Taking a single x-ray could take up to 90 minutes, which meant the patient had to remain completely still for a long time while being exposed to dangerous levels of radiation. (1 mark)

5.2.q: 1 (a). Describe one feature of the support trench system on the Western Front. (2 marks)
5.2.m: Model Answer: One feature of the support trench system was that it was located approximately 80 metres behind the frontline. This provided a safer area where troops could retreat if the frontline trench came under heavy enemy attack.

5.2.q: 1 (b). Describe one feature of the problems involved in transporting wounded soldiers away from the battleground. (2 marks)
5.2.m:
Model Answer: One feature of the transport problems was the devastated, muddy terrain. Constant artillery shelling destroyed the roads and drainage, creating deep mud that caused motor ambulances to get stuck and made it incredibly difficult for stretcher-bearers to carry the wounded.
________________

5.2.q: 2 (a). How useful are Sources A and B for an enquiry into the impact of the terrain on the transport of the wounded on the Western Front? Explain your answer, using Sources A and B and your knowledge of the historical context. (8 marks)
5.2.m:
Model Answer: Source A is highly useful for an enquiry into the impact of the terrain on transporting the wounded because its content vividly details the physical difficulty caused by the environment. It reveals that the mud was "waist deep," which meant stretcher-bearers became rapidly "exhausted" and could only rescue "two or three wounded men" a day. I can support this with my own contextual knowledge: constant artillery bombardment had destroyed the region's natural drainage, creating a waterlogged landscape of deep mud and flooded shell craters. The complex, zig-zag design of the trenches also made it incredibly difficult to manoeuvre stretchers without jolting the patient. Furthermore, the provenance makes the source incredibly useful; as a recorded memory from an East Anglian Field Ambulance worker who was actually carrying stretchers at Ypres, it gives a reliable, eyewitness insight into the severe physical toll the mud took on the men trying to save lives. Overall, Source A provides highly useful, accurate evidence of how the terrain critically slowed down the manual evacuation of the wounded.
Source B is also highly useful for this enquiry because its content provides direct visual evidence of the types of transport the medical services were forced to rely on due to the ruined terrain. It shows a basic, horse-drawn ambulance wagon navigating a muddy, rutted track. My contextual knowledge confirms that although motor ambulances were deployed during the war, heavy motor vehicles frequently got stuck in the deep mud. This forced the RAMC to continue relying heavily on horse-drawn wagons—sometimes requiring up to six horses to pull them through the sludge—which were much bumpier and often worsened the severe injuries of the casualties inside. The provenance makes the source useful as it is an official photograph taken precisely at the time of the Battle of the Somme in September 1916, providing an objective, literal snapshot of the difficult, muddy environment the Field Ambulance had to operate in. Therefore, Source B is extremely useful as it demonstrates why modern motor ambulances could not entirely replace slower, bumpier horse-drawn transport on the muddy Western Front.
________________

5.3.q: 2 (b). How could you follow up Source A to find out more about the impact of the terrain on the transport of the wounded on the Western Front? In your answer, you must give the question you would ask and the type of source you could use. (4 marks)
5.3.m:
Model Answer:
* Detail in Source A that I would follow up: "If you got two or three wounded men down in a day, that was all you could expect to do." (1 mark)
* Question I would ask: What was the average number of casualties a stretcher-bearer team managed to transport in a single day at Ypres in 1917? (1 mark)
* What type of source I could use: Official RAMC (Royal Army Medical Corps) war diaries or unit records for stretcher-bearer companies operating at Ypres in 1917. (1 mark)
* How this might help answer my question: These official records would detail the exact number of daily operations and the total number of wounded men transported by the teams each day, allowing me to calculate if the extreme mud truly limited most teams to just two or three rescues a day. (1 mark)

5.3.q: 1 (a). Describe one feature of trench foot on the Western Front. (2 marks)
5.3.m: Model Answer: One feature of trench foot was the painful swelling and rotting of the flesh. This was caused by standing for long periods in cold, waterlogged mud, which restricted blood flow and led to gangrene.

5.3.q: 1 (b). Describe one feature of gas attacks on the Western Front. (2 marks)
5.3.m:
Model Answer: One feature of gas attacks was the use of deadly chlorine gas. This was first used by the Germans at the Second Battle of Ypres in 1915 and caused death by slowly suffocating the victim's lungs.
________________

5.3.q: 2 (a). How useful are Sources A and B for an enquiry into the effects of poison gas attacks on soldiers on the Western Front? Explain your answer, using Sources A and B and your knowledge of the historical context. (8 marks)
5.3.m:
Model Answer: Source A is highly useful for an enquiry into the effects of gas attacks because its content vividly details the horrific physical symptoms it caused. It reveals that chlorine gas caused "a flooding of the lungs" and the "coughing up of a greenish froth", ultimately "resulting in death". I can support this with my own contextual knowledge: chlorine gas was first used as a weapon at the Second Battle of Ypres in 1915 and worked by suffocating the victim from the inside out, causing massive panic among the troops who were completely unprepared for it. Furthermore, the provenance makes the source incredibly useful; it is a first-hand notebook account from Lance Sergeant Elmer Cotton who served during 1915, making it a reliable, eyewitness record of the sheer terror and physical trauma the gas inflicted on the men. Overall, Source A provides highly useful, accurate evidence of the deadly physical impact of early gas attacks.
Source B is also highly useful for this enquiry because its content provides direct evidence of the lethal effectiveness and visual impact of gas on the victims. It describes a "huge, greenish cloud of smoke" that caused a man's face to turn "dark blue" as he was "struggling to breathe". My contextual knowledge confirms that chlorine gas formed a visible green cloud that destroyed the lungs, and because the British army did not issue proper gas masks until July 1915, casualty rates in early attacks could be devastatingly high. The provenance makes the source useful as it is a personal account from a soldier who directly witnessed the aftermath of the attack at the ambulances, providing a raw, emotional insight into the suffering. Therefore, Source B is extremely useful as it demonstrates exactly how vulnerable the soldiers initially were to the suffocating effects of this terrifying new weapon.
________________

5.4.q: 2 (b). How could you follow up Source A to find out more about the effects of poison gas attacks on soldiers on the Western Front? In your answer, you must give the question you would ask and the type of source you could use. (4 marks)
5.4.m:
Model Answer:
* Detail in Source A that I would follow up: "...finally resulting in death. It is a fiendish death to die." (1 mark)
* Question I would ask: What percentage of British soldiers who were exposed to gas attacks actually died from them on the Western Front? (1 mark)
* What type of source I could use: Official RAMC (Royal Army Medical Corps) medical statistics and casualty survival rate records for the British army between 1915 and 1918. (1 mark)
* How this might help answer my question: These official statistics would provide the exact numerical data required to see if gas was actually as broadly lethal as the terrifying eyewitness accounts suggest, or if the rapid introduction of gas masks successfully reduced the overall death rate to a low percentage. (1 mark)

5.4.q: 1 (a). Describe one feature of the work of the First Aid Nursing Yeomanry (FANY). (2 marks)
5.4.m: Model Answer: One feature of the work of the FANY was driving motor ambulances. (1 mark) As a women's voluntary organisation, they frequently drove ambulances to transport wounded men from the frontline to hospitals, as well as driving supplies and mobile bath units. (1 mark)

5.4.q: 1 (b). Describe one feature of the Casualty Clearing Stations (CCS). (2 marks)
5.4.m:
Model Answer: One feature of the CCS was the use of the triage system. (1 mark) When wounded soldiers arrived, medical staff used triage to sort them into three groups—such as the walking wounded and those needing immediate surgery—to prioritise life-saving care. (1 mark)
________________

5.4.q: 2 (a). How useful are Sources A and B for an enquiry into the problems of transporting the wounded on the Western Front? Explain your answer, using Sources A and B and your knowledge of the historical context. (8 marks)
5.4.m:
Model Answer: Source A is highly useful for an enquiry into the transport of the wounded because its content highlights the severe physical distress caused to patients by the poor transport infrastructure. It describes the journeys as "perfect nightmares" because it was "impossible not to bump" over the "appalling roads full of holes". I can support this with my own contextual knowledge: constant artillery shelling completely destroyed the roads and terrain on the Western Front, meaning ambulance journeys were violently bumpy. This shaking frequently worsened soldiers' injuries and increased fatal blood loss before they could reach a Casualty Clearing Station for surgery. The provenance makes the source extremely useful; as a published autobiography from an actual FANY ambulance driver who worked during the 1916 Somme offensive, it provides a highly reliable, eyewitness account of the emotional and physical reality of evacuating the wounded over devastated terrain. Overall, Source A provides highly useful evidence of how poor roads made motor transport agonizing for the wounded.
Source B is also highly useful for this enquiry because its content provides direct visual evidence of the types of transport the medical services were forced to rely on due to the terrible conditions. It shows a basic, horse-drawn ambulance wagon navigating a muddy, rutted track. My contextual knowledge confirms that although 512 motor ambulances were introduced after a public appeal in 1914, heavy motor vehicles frequently got stuck in the deep mud. This forced the RAMC to continue relying heavily on horse-drawn wagons—sometimes requiring up to six horses to pull them through the sludge—which were much slower and bumpier for the casualties inside. The provenance makes the source useful as it is a photograph taken precisely at the time of the Battle of the Somme in September 1916, providing an objective, literal snapshot of the difficult, muddy environment the Field Ambulance had to operate in. Therefore, Source B is extremely useful as it demonstrates why modern motor ambulances could not entirely replace slower horse-drawn transport on the muddy Western Front.
________________

5.5.q: 2 (b). How could you follow up Source A to find out more about the problems of transporting the wounded on the Western Front? In your answer, you must give the question you would ask and the type of source you could use. (4 marks)
5.5.m:
Model Answer:
* Detail in Source A that I would follow up: "...it was impossible not to bump a certain amount over those appalling roads full of holes and cobbles." (1 mark)
* Question I would ask: How frequently did the bumpy ambulance transport over ruined roads directly cause soldiers to die from shock or blood loss before they could reach the Casualty Clearing Station? (1 mark)
* What type of source I could use: Official RAMC medical admission and mortality registers from Casualty Clearing Stations operating during the Battle of the Somme in 1916. (1 mark)
* How this might help answer my question: These official registers would provide the exact statistical data needed to see the condition of patients upon arrival, showing whether a high percentage of men were arriving dead or in severe shock specifically due to blood loss worsened during the bumpy ambulance journey. (1 mark)

5.5.q: 1 (a). Describe one feature of the Thomas splint on the Western Front. (2 marks)
5.5.m: Model Answer: One feature of the Thomas splint was that it kept the leg perfectly rigid during transport. (1 mark) This prevented the broken bone from causing further internal damage, which successfully reduced fatal blood loss and shock. (1 mark)

5.5.q: 1 (b). Describe one feature of the use of blood transfusions on the Western Front. (2 marks)
5.5.m:
Model Answer: One feature of blood transfusions was that new chemical techniques allowed blood to be stored for the first time. (1 mark) For example, the addition of citrate glucose allowed Oswald Hope Robertson to store blood and create the first 'blood bank' before the Battle of Cambrai in 1917. (1 mark)
________________

5.5.q: 2 (a). How useful are Sources A and B for an enquiry into new medical techniques being used on the Western Front to deal with injuries? Explain your answer, using Sources A and B and your knowledge of the historical context. (8 marks)
5.5.m:
Model Answer: Source A is highly useful for an enquiry into new medical techniques because its content details the dramatic success of the Carrel-Dakin method in fighting infection. It states that the technique reduced gas gangrene infection rates from 80% down to just 20%. I can support this with my own contextual knowledge: because traditional aseptic surgery was impossible in dirty casualty stations and standard carbolic acid failed to kill deeply embedded gas gangrene, the Carrel-Dakin method was developed to pump a sterilised salt solution directly into deep wounds through rubber tubes to continuously wash out the bacteria. The provenance makes the source useful because, although it is a modern medical journal from 2019, it has the benefit of hindsight and statistical research to objectively judge how much of a "major breakthrough" the technique was compared to earlier battles. Overall, Source A provides highly useful, statistical evidence of how chemical irrigation successfully prevented lethal infections.
Source B is also highly useful for this enquiry because its content provides direct visual evidence of how diagnostic technology was adapted to deal with complex injuries on the battlefield. It shows a mobile x-ray machine operating out of a tent attached to the back of a van. My contextual knowledge confirms that x-rays were completely essential for accurately locating bullets and shrapnel before surgery so they could be removed to prevent gangrene. Because static machines were large and difficult to move, six mobile units were developed for the British sector so they could be driven directly to Casualty Clearing Stations, allowing surgeons to identify injuries closer to the fighting. The provenance makes the source useful as it is a photograph taken at the time in 1917, providing an accurate, literal snapshot of how these complex electrical machines were actually deployed and powered by the van's engine. Therefore, Source B is extremely useful as it demonstrates how high-tech medical equipment was successfully made mobile to save lives at the front.
________________

5.5.q:
Q2. Explain why the government took action to improve public health in the period c1900–present. (12 marks) You may use the following in your answer:
* Lung cancer
* The NHS You must also use information of your own.
* Teacher Note on Exam Technique:
* For Q1 (4 marks): Focus on technology vs. observation. Industrial detail: Doctors diagnosing by looking at external symptoms or using basic stethoscopes, unable to see inside without cutting. Modern detail: Using highly advanced, non-invasive technology like CT scans or PET-CT scans to pinpoint exact tumours at a cellular level.
* For Q2 (12 marks): Paragraph 1: The discovery of lifestyle factors (Explaining that scientific research linked smoking to lung cancer, forcing the government to ban smoking in 2007 to prevent deaths). Paragraph 2: The creation of the NHS (The government realized the poor could not afford care, launching the NHS in 1948 to provide cradle-to-grave care). Paragraph 3 (Own Knowledge): The cost of healthcare (Treating preventable diseases like lung cancer or obesity is incredibly expensive for the NHS, so the government introduced the Sugar Tax and plain packaging to save money by preventing the illnesses before they happen).
* From <https://notebooklm.google.com/notebook/17197bf0-83c5-43f4-8f07-5aa0f068c468>

1.1.q: 2 (b). How could you follow up Source A to find out more about new medical techniques being used on the Western Front to deal with injuries? In your answer, you must give the question you would ask and the type of source you could use. (4 marks)
1.1.m:
Model Answer:
* Detail in Source A that I would follow up: "...when surgeons applied the Carrel-Dakin technique during the Battle of the Somme, that number was 20%." (1 mark)
* Question I would ask: Did the alternative new surgical technique of 'debridement' (wound excision) result in an even lower gangrene infection rate than the Carrel-Dakin method during the Battle of the Somme? (1 mark)
* What type of source I could use: Official Army Medical Records with statistical data detailing the survival and infection rates of men who underwent debridement surgery at Casualty Clearing Stations in 1916. (1 mark)
* How this might help answer my question: These official records would provide the exact comparative statistics needed to see if physically cutting away the infected tissue (debridement) was actually a more effective new technique for preventing gangrene than flushing the wound with salt solution. (1 mark)

1.1.q: Q1. Explain one way in which ideas about the cause of disease in the medieval period (c1250–c1500) were similar to ideas about the cause of disease in the Renaissance period (c1500–c1700). (4 marks)

1.2.q:
Q2. ‘The Theory of the Four Humours was the main idea about the cause of disease in the Middle Ages’. How far do you agree? Explain your answer. (16 marks) You may use the following in your answer:
* Galen
* the Church You must also use information of your own.
* Teacher Note on Exam Technique:
* For Q1 (4 marks): Remind students to clearly identify the similarity in the very first sentence (e.g., "One similarity was the continued belief in the miasma theory"). They must then provide one piece of specific factual evidence from the medieval period, and one piece of specific factual evidence from the Renaissance period to prove the similarity.
* For Q2 (16 marks): Students must evaluate the statement. They should dedicate one paragraph to agreeing with the statement (explaining how Galen and the Church kept the Four Humours dominant). They must then write at least one or two paragraphs disagreeing by bringing in alternative ideas about causes (e.g., God/sin, astrology, and miasma). A strong conclusion must weigh up these factors and make a definitive judgement on "how far" they agree. Ensure they include 'information of their own' outside of the stimulus bullet points to access Level 4.
* From <https://notebooklm.google.com/notebook/17197bf0-83c5-43f4-8f07-5aa0f068c468>
Because generating all 21 remaining topics at once would severely exceed the system's output limits, I will provide these highly detailed frameworks in logical batches. Here is the next batch containing the rest of the Medieval period and the start of the Medical Renaissance. Just say "continue" when you are ready for the next set!
Q1. Explain one way in which the care of the sick in the medieval period (c1250–c1500) was similar to the care of the sick in the Renaissance period (c1500–c1700). (4 marks)

1.3.q:
Q2. ‘In the years c1250–c1500, the physician was the most important person providing care and treatment.’ How far do you agree? Explain your answer. (16 marks) You may use the following in your answer:
* medical training
* herbal remedies You must also use information of your own.
* Teacher Note on Exam Technique:
* For Q1 (4 marks): Students should explicitly state the similarity in the opening sentence (e.g., "One similarity was that the vast majority of sick people were cared for at home by women using herbal remedies"). They must provide one specific supporting fact from the Medieval era, and one from the Renaissance era to access all 4 marks.
* For Q2 (16 marks): Students must debate the word "most important". Paragraph 1: Agree (Physicians had university training, read Galen, and directed apothecaries). Paragraphs 2 & 3: Disagree (Physicians were too expensive for ordinary people; women in the home and cheaper apothecaries/barber-surgeons actually provided the majority of the nation's care). Conclusion: A clear judgement on "how far" they agree, weighing up the official authority of physicians versus the practical reality of home care.
Q1. Explain one way in which people’s reactions to the Black Death (1348) were similar to people's reactions to the Great Plague (1665). (4 marks)

2.1.q:
Q2. ‘The main reason why medical care and treatment was ineffective during the medieval period was because medical knowledge was based on Galen’s ideas.’ How far do you agree? Explain your answer. (16 marks) You may use the following in your answer:
* Theory of Opposites
* hospitals You must also use information of your own.
* Teacher Note on Exam Technique:
* For Q1 (4 marks): Students can focus on either Religious reactions (praying/fasting as it was a punishment from God) or Miasma reactions (carrying sweet-smelling herbs to ward off bad air). Specific evidence from both 1348 and 1665 is required.
* For Q2 (16 marks): Encourage students to use the Black Death as their "information of their own" to argue against the statement. They can argue that the absolute lack of scientific technology (microscopes/Germ Theory) and the overpowering belief in supernatural causes (God's punishment) were just as responsible for ineffective treatments as Galen's Four Humours.
Q1. Explain one way in which ideas about the cause of disease in the medieval period (c1250–c1500) were similar to ideas about the cause of disease in the Renaissance period (c1500–c1700). (4 marks)

2.2.q:
Q2. Explain why there were changes in the way ideas about the causes of disease and illness were communicated in the period c1500–c1700. (12 marks) You may use the following in your answer:
* the printing press
* the Royal Society You must also use information of your own.
* Teacher Note on Exam Technique:
* For Q1 (4 marks): Students should identify the continued belief in Miasma or God's punishment. They must give one specific detail from the Medieval era (e.g., blaming miasma for the 1348 Black Death) and one from the Renaissance (e.g., still lighting street fires to fight miasma during the 1665 Great Plague).
* For Q2 (12 marks): This is a pure causation question focused entirely on communication. Paragraph 1: The Printing Press (mass production of accurate texts and illustrations without Church censorship). Paragraph 2: The Royal Society (the creation of Philosophical Transactions allowing European scientists to read about and verify each other's experiments). Paragraph 3 (Own Information): They could mention the rise in literacy rates and Humanism, which created a wider audience eager to read and communicate new scientific ideas.

Here is the next batch of Retrieval-Based Lesson Frameworks, covering the rest of the Medical Renaissance and the start of the Industrial Period. Just say "continue" when you are ready for the next set!

Q1. Explain one way in which the approaches to hospital care in the medieval period (c1250–c1500) were different from the approaches to hospital care in the Renaissance period (c1500–c1700). (4 marks)

2.3.q:
Q2. Explain why there was rapid progress in anatomical knowledge in the years c1500–c1700. (12 marks) You may use the following in your answer:
* Andreas Vesalius
* the printing press You must also use information of your own.
* Teacher Note on Exam Technique:
* For Q1 (4 marks): Students should identify the shift from general hospitality/prayer to the isolation of contagious disease. Evidence 1: Medieval Church hospitals rejected infectious patients and offered "care, not cure". Evidence 2: Renaissance Pest Houses were specifically built to isolate contagious diseases like the plague away from the healthy population.
* For Q2 (12 marks): Paragraph 1: The role of key individuals (Vesalius dissecting bodies himself and proving Galen wrong). Paragraph 2: Technology (The printing press allowing Vesalius' detailed, accurate diagrams to be widely shared without Church censorship). Paragraph 3 (Own Knowledge): The decline of Church power/rise of Humanism, which encouraged scientists to dissect human bodies rather than just reading ancient texts.
Q1. Explain one way in which people’s reactions to the Black Death (1348) were similar to people's reactions to the Great Plague (1665). (4 marks)

3.1.q:
Q2. ‘The most effective method of preventing the spread of the Great Plague (1665) was the use of quarantine.’ How far do you agree? Explain your answer. (16 marks) You may use the following in your answer:
* Watchmen
* Flight from the city You must also use information of your own.
* Teacher Note on Exam Technique:
* For Q1 (4 marks): Students can focus on Miasma reactions (carrying herbs, lighting fires) or Religious reactions (praying, fasting). They must provide one specific detail from 1348 and one specific detail from 1665 to secure full marks.
* For Q2 (16 marks): Evaluate the statement. Paragraph 1: Agree (Quarantine/Watchmen successfully locked away infected people, showing an understanding of contagion). Paragraph 2: Disagree - Flight (Running away was actually the only 100% effective method of survival, as used by the King). Paragraph 3: Disagree - Government failures (The animal cull actively worsened the plague, and lighting street fires did nothing to stop fleas). Conclusion: Weigh up the brutal effectiveness of quarantine vs. flight.

Q1. Explain one way in which ideas about the cause of disease in the industrial period (c1700–c1900) were different from ideas about the cause of disease in the medieval period (c1250–c1500). (4 marks)

3.2.q:
Q2. ‘Louis Pasteur’s publication of the Germ Theory was the biggest turning point in understanding the causes of disease.’ How far do you agree? Explain your answer. (16 marks) You may use the following in your answer:
* Spontaneous Generation
* Robert Koch You must also use information of your own.
* Teacher Note on Exam Technique:
* For Q1 (4 marks): Students should contrast the scientific vs. supernatural approach. Medieval detail: Relying on God or astrology (e.g., 1345 planet alignment). Industrial detail: Relying on science and microscopes (e.g., Pasteur proving microbes caused disease in 1861).
* For Q2 (16 marks): Evaluate the statement. Paragraph 1: Agree (Pasteur destroyed Spontaneous Generation and provided the foundation for all modern medicine). Paragraph 2: Disagree - Koch (Pasteur only proved germs caused decay; it was Koch who proved specific germs caused specific human diseases like TB, which was a bigger practical turning point for doctors). Paragraph 3: Disagree - DNA (The 1953 discovery of DNA by Crick and Watson was a bigger turning point for understanding genetic, non-infectious diseases). Conclusion: A clear judgement weighing up Pasteur's foundational theory against Koch's practical application.
* From <https://notebooklm.google.com/notebook/17197bf0-83c5-43f4-8f07-5aa0f068c468>
Here is the next batch of Retrieval-Based Lesson Frameworks, covering the monumental medical breakthroughs of the Industrial Period and the shift into Modern scientific diagnosis.
Just say "continue" when you are ready for the next set!
Q1. Explain one way in which hospital care in the industrial period (c1700–c1900) was different from hospital care in the medieval period (c1250–c1500). (4 marks)

3.3.q:
Q2. ‘The discovery of Carbolic Acid was the most significant turning point in surgery in the years c1700–c1900.’ How far do you agree? Explain your answer. (16 marks) You may use the following in your answer:
* Joseph Lister
* James Simpson You must also use information of your own.
* Teacher Note on Exam Technique:
* For Q1 (4 marks): Students should contrast the purpose of the hospital. Medieval: Run by monks/nuns, focusing on "care, not cure" (hospitality/prayer) and rejecting infectious patients. Industrial: Focus shifted to professional medical treatment, hygiene (Nightingale's Pavilion plan), and curing patients using new medicines/antiseptics.
* For Q2 (16 marks): Evaluate the turning points. Paragraph 1: Agree (Lister's carbolic acid solved infection, the biggest killer, making surgery survivable). Paragraph 2: Disagree - Pain (Simpson's chloroform was the first barrier broken, allowing complex internal surgery). Paragraph 3: Disagree - Blood Loss/Science (Note that blood loss remained unsolved until 1901, or argue that Pasteur's Germ Theory was the true turning point because Lister's work relied entirely upon it).
Q1. Explain one way in which people’s reactions to the Black Death (1348) were similar to people's reactions to Cholera (1854). (4 marks)

4.1.q:
Q2. Explain why there was rapid progress in public health in the second half of the nineteenth century (c1850–c1900). (12 marks) You may use the following in your answer:
* John Snow
* The Great Stink You must also use information of your own.
* Teacher Note on Exam Technique:
* For Q1 (4 marks): Similarity = Blaming bad air (Miasma). 1348 detail: Lighting fires and carrying herbs to drive away the plague miasma. 1854 detail: Blaming the stench of the slums and the Thames for cholera, prompting them to try and clean up the smells rather than the water.
* For Q2 (12 marks): Causation question. Paragraph 1: John Snow (Proving cholera was waterborne via the Broad Street pump map). Paragraph 2: The Great Stink (The terrible smell of the Thames breaking the government's laissez-faire attitude, leading to Bazalgette's sewers). Paragraph 3 (Own Knowledge): Louis Pasteur's Germ Theory (1861) or the 1875 Public Health Act, explaining how scientific proof of microbes forced councils to finally provide clean water.

Q1. Explain one way in which the methods used to diagnose illness in the medieval period (c1250–c1500) were different from the methods used to diagnose illness in the modern period (c1900–present). (4 marks)

4.2.q:
Q2. ‘The discovery of DNA was the most significant breakthrough in understanding the causes of illness in the period c1900–present.’ How far do you agree? Explain your answer. (16 marks) You may use the following in your answer:
* Watson and Crick
* Lifestyle factors You must also use information of your own.
* Teacher Note on Exam Technique:
* For Q1 (4 marks): Contrast technology. Medieval detail: Relying entirely on visual symptoms, astrology (Zodiac charts), or tasting/smelling urine charts. Modern detail: Using high-tech scans like MRIs or laboratory blood tests to precisely identify internal problems without guessing.
* For Q2 (16 marks): Evaluate the breakthrough. Paragraph 1: Agree (DNA solved the mystery of hereditary diseases that Germ theory couldn't explain; led to the Genome Project). Paragraph 2: Disagree - Lifestyle Factors (Understanding that smoking causes lung cancer or diet causes diabetes has saved millions of lives practically). Paragraph 3: Disagree - High Tech Diagnosis (Scans/blood tests allow for the actual detection of these diseases). Conclusion: Weigh up the theoretical brilliance of DNA vs. the practical impact of lifestyle and diagnostic scans.
* From <https://notebooklm.google.com/notebook/17197bf0-83c5-43f4-8f07-5aa0f068c468>
Here is the next batch of Retrieval-Based Lesson Frameworks, covering the massive medical transformations of the 20th century.
Just say "continue" when you are ready to move on to the final section: The British Sector of the Western Front!
Q1. Explain one way in which the treatments for illness in the industrial period (c1700–c1900) were different from the treatments for illness in the modern period (c1900–present). (4 marks)

4.3.q:
Q2. ‘Alexander Fleming was the most important individual in the development of penicillin.’ How far do you agree? Explain your answer. (16 marks) You may use the following in your answer:
* Staphylococcus
* Mass production You must also use information of your own.
* Teacher Note on Exam Technique:
* For Q1 (4 marks): Focus on the shift from treating symptoms to killing internal microbes. Industrial detail: Using herbal remedies or resting (Nightingale's hospitals) because they had no drugs to kill internal germs. Modern detail: Using targeted antibiotics like Penicillin to actively hunt and destroy bacteria inside the bloodstream.
* For Q2 (16 marks): Evaluate the individuals. Paragraph 1: Agree (Fleming discovered it, noticed the mould killed staphylococcus, and published the initial paper). Paragraph 2: Disagree - Florey and Chain (They were the ones who actually proved it worked on mice/humans and purified it into a usable drug). Paragraph 3: Disagree - Government/Institutions (The US Government funded the mass production for WWII; without this money, it would have remained a laboratory experiment). Conclusion: Fleming provided the raw science, but Florey/Chain provided the practical medicine.

Q1. Explain one way in which the government’s approach to public health in the industrial period (c1700–c1900) was similar to the government’s approach to public health in the modern period (c1900–present). (4 marks)

4.4.q:
Q2. Explain why there was rapid progress in disease prevention after 1900. (12 marks) You may use the following in your answer:
* Government vaccination campaigns
* Clean Air Acts You must also use information of your own.
* Teacher Note on Exam Technique:
* For Q1 (4 marks): Focus on the government forcing change through laws. Industrial detail: The 1875 Public Health Act forced councils to build sewers to stop cholera. Modern detail: The 1956 Clean Air Act or the 2007 Smoking Ban legally forced people to change behaviours to prevent lung/breathing diseases.
* For Q2 (12 marks): Causation focus. Paragraph 1: Mass Vaccinations (The government actively funding nationwide polio/diphtheria vaccines rather than leaving it to chance). Paragraph 2: Legislation (Passing the Clean Air Acts to remove smog/environmental triggers). Paragraph 3 (Own Knowledge): DNA and Lifestyle understanding (Proving that smoking causes lung cancer, leading to the government launching advertising campaigns and the 'Sugar Tax' to prevent disease before it requires hospital treatment).

Q1. Explain one way in which the methods used to diagnose illness in the industrial period (c1700–c1900) were different from the methods used to diagnose illness in the modern period (c1900–present). (4 marks)
